topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, sharp and unforgiving against my already frayed nerves. I stood at the edge of the room, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white islands in the deepening shadows. There, hanging on the pristine white wall, was 'Ephemeral Echoes'—my collection. Each brushstroke, every carefully layered pigment, was now public property, a silent accusation. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat, a sound that tasted like ash. I watched a woman walk past, her laughter bright and utterly disconnected from the heavy atmosphere I carried. My feet felt rooted to the cold marble floor; I couldn't move, couldn't breathe past the tightness constricting my chest. The silence of the room amplified the roaring in my ears, a constant, unwelcome soundtrack to this profound violation.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing the studio floor, the turpentine fumes doing little to clear the fog in my head. Every time my gaze fell upon the canvases—my vulnerable confessions—a wave of nausea would wash over me, leaving me shaky and cold. It wasn't anger that fueled my tremor; it was a hollow ache, a vast emptiness where my creative spirit used to reside. I ran a hand over my face, feeling the dampness of unshed tears prickle at my eyes, but I refused to let them fall. "This isn't right," I whispered to the empty space, the words thin and brittle. The sheer audacity of it—seeing my soul laid bare and treated as mere decoration—made my stomach clench painfully. I retreated to a corner, pulling my knees to my chest, wishing I could simply dissolve into the rough canvas beneath me.

[story 3]

The velvet rope felt like a physical barrier separating me from the spectacle. I kept replaying the moment I saw the catalogue photos—my pieces, framed and cataloged, bathed in flattering spotlights. A slow, heavy slump settled into my shoulders, dragging my spine down. I tried to approach the display, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, turning to dry, useless gravel. My vision blurred at the edges, and the vibrant colours of the exhibited work seemed to mock the muted, grey landscape of my internal world. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot, solitary path down my cheek, instantly chilling against my skin. I closed my eyes, focusing on the dull throb behind my ribs, a constant, heavy pressure that refused to ease.
